passages.title: The Hidden Garden
passages.text:
When Arun followed the narrow path through Briar Wood, he expected it to end at the river. Instead, it bent sharply behind a clump of hazel trees and led him to a stone arch he had never seen before.

The arch was half covered in ivy. At its centre hung a rusted iron ring. Arun brushed away the leaves and pulled. With a groan, the wooden gate beneath the ivy swung inward.

Beyond it was a walled garden.

Beds of mint and lavender grew in neat squares. A cracked sundial leaned to one side, and a tiny pond reflected a slice of grey sky. Nothing moved except a robin pecking at the path.

Arun took one careful step inside. Whoever had made this place had once cared for it deeply. Even now, although weeds pushed through the gravel, the garden did not feel wild. It felt waiting.

At the far end stood a glasshouse with clouded panes. Arun could just make out the shapes of shelves inside. He glanced back towards the wood. The sensible thing would be to fetch his sister and come back later.

Yet the garden seemed to have kept its secret for years, and for one bright moment he wanted the secret all to himself.

passages.title: How Sundials Work
passages.text:
A sundial tells the time by using the position of the Sun. Long before clocks became common, people used sundials in gardens, courtyards and town squares.

At the centre of a sundial is a raised piece called a gnomon. When sunlight falls on it, the gnomon casts a shadow. As the Sun appears to move across the sky, the shadow shifts. The markings on the dial show the hour.

Sundials work best on bright days. On cloudy days, the shadow may be faint or disappear completely. They also need to be placed carefully so they point in the correct direction. A badly positioned sundial will give the wrong time.

Although sundials are simple, they helped people organise daily life for hundreds of years. Today, they are still used in some gardens and schools, not because they are the most accurate timekeepers, but because they show how people once measured time using nature.

passages.title: At the Bird Hide
passages.text:
By the time the school minibus reached the nature reserve, the sky had already turned the colour of wet slate. Leila pressed her forehead to the window and watched reeds shiver beside the lake.

“Ten minutes,” said Mr Kemp, “and then we head for the bird hide.”

The class hurried out with their waterproofs zipped to the chin. At first, the rain was only a mist. Then the wind arrived.

It came skimming across the water, flattening the reeds and pushing ripples towards the shore. A loose information board banged against its post. Somewhere above them, unseen geese called sharply.

Leila and her partner, Ben, ducked into the hide just as the rain thickened. Inside, it smelled of damp wood and mud. Through the narrow window, the lake looked completely different now—darker, rougher, almost angry.

Ben pointed to a cluster of black dots near the far bank. “Coots,” he whispered.

Leila raised her binoculars, but a sudden blast rattled the wooden wall. She jumped, then laughed at herself.

Strangely, the storm made everything easier to notice: the silver slant of rain, the white flash of a bird’s wing, the way the clouds seemed to lean low over the water. The reserve no longer felt like a quiet outing. It felt like a place with its own weather and its own mood.

passages.title: How Storms Form
passages.text:
Storm clouds form when warm, moist air rises and cools. As it cools, the water vapour in the air turns into tiny droplets, which gather to form clouds.

If the air keeps rising, the cloud can grow taller and denser. In some storms, the top of the cloud reaches very high into the atmosphere. These giant clouds can produce heavy rain, thunder, lightning and strong winds.

Wind often becomes stronger before and during a storm because air is moving quickly from one place to another. Differences in temperature and pressure can cause this movement. Over lakes or open fields, strong wind may feel even more powerful because there are fewer buildings or trees to slow it down.

Meteorologists, who study weather, use radar and satellites to track storms. This helps them warn people when severe weather is approaching. Although storms can be dramatic to watch, they can also be dangerous, so it is important to listen to weather warnings and take shelter if needed.

passages.title: The Blue Door
passages.text:
The note was folded into a square so small that Tomas nearly missed it. It had been pushed beneath the lid of the old biscuit tin where his grandmother kept buttons, bits of string and odd keys that opened nothing.

He unfolded the paper carefully.

Find the blue door before sunset.

There was no name, no map and no explanation.

Tomas looked around the attic. Dust floated through a beam of light from the roof window. Trunks stood against one wall; picture frames leaned against another. There was certainly no blue door.

He almost laughed. Then he noticed that one of the trunks had left a pale rectangle on the floorboards, as though it had not always stood where it was now. With effort, he dragged it aside.

Beneath it was a narrow trapdoor with a round handle painted blue.

A shiver ran along Tomas’s arms. The note had not been a game after all.

He knelt and lifted the trapdoor. A cool draft rose from below, carrying the smell of stone and rain. Steps disappeared into darkness.

Grandma was downstairs hanging washing in the garden. Tomas could call her. He probably should. Yet the attic, which had seemed ordinary just minutes earlier, now felt full of unfinished stories. Gripping the note in one hand, he placed his foot on the first step.

passages.title: Codes and Clues
passages.text:
Codes and clues have been used for centuries to keep messages private or to guide people to a hidden place. A simple clue might describe an object or place in a puzzling way. The reader must work out what the clue really means.

Treasure hunts often use a chain of clues. One clue leads to the next, and each step reveals a little more information. This makes the challenge more exciting because the person solving it must notice details, make sensible guesses and test ideas.

More secret systems, such as ciphers, change the letters in a message according to a pattern. For example, each letter might be moved forward by one place in the alphabet. A becomes B, B becomes C, and so on. Without knowing the pattern, the message can be difficult to read.

Today, codes are still used in different ways. Computers use complex codes to protect information, while games and escape rooms use clues and puzzles to create mystery and fun.

passages.title: The Vixen Under the Bench
passages.text:
The fox appeared just as Nia was clipping the dead heads from the marigolds. It slipped between the compost bin and the fence, paused beneath the apple tree and stared straight at her.

For a second, neither of them moved.

Then Nia noticed the fox was limping.

Its left front paw barely touched the ground. The animal looked thinner than she expected too, with a ragged patch of fur near one shoulder. It turned to leave, but not before glancing back towards the shed.

Nia followed its gaze.

Beneath the old wooden bench beside the shed was a dark shape. She crept closer. Two tiny fox cubs were curled together in a nest of dry leaves. One lifted its head and made a weak, squeaking sound.

Nia’s breath caught. The vixen had brought her cubs into the garden.

She backed away slowly and called for Dad. Within minutes, the two of them were peering from the kitchen window. Dad frowned. “We shouldn’t go too near,” he said. “If she feels trapped, she may not come back.”

Together they phoned a local wildlife rescue centre. The volunteer who answered asked careful questions and told them to keep the garden quiet, leave fresh water nearby and watch from a distance.

All afternoon, Nia checked the window. At dusk, the fox returned, moving carefully but carrying food in her mouth. Nia felt relieved. The garden, which had always been tidy and ordinary, had suddenly become part of a wild and secret life.

passages.title: Living with Urban Wildlife
passages.text:
Urban wildlife is the name given to wild animals that live in towns and cities. Although many people think of cities as crowded places made of roads and buildings, they can still provide food, shelter and safe routes for animals.

Foxes are one example of an animal that has adapted well to urban life. Gardens may offer quiet places to rest, while bins and compost heaps can provide food. Hedgehogs, blackbirds, squirrels and some bats also make use of parks, hedges and even small gaps between fences.

People can help urban wildlife by planting flowers, leaving shallow dishes of water, avoiding litter and making spaces easier for animals to move through. For example, small holes in garden fences can allow hedgehogs to travel safely from one garden to another.

Living alongside wild animals can be exciting, but it also means acting responsibly. Animals should not be crowded or handled unless trained experts advise it. Watching quietly from a distance is often the best way to help.

passages.title: The Model Ship
passages.text:
The museum's upstairs gallery was almost empty when Samira slipped inside. She had come to sketch the old ship figurehead for her art homework, but something else caught her eye first.

At the far end of the room stood a model of a polar ship trapped in painted ice. Its tiny ropes and sails were covered in dust. A label beside it read:
Endurance – Antarctic Expedition

Samira leaned closer. The ship looked sturdy, yet the ice around it rose in jagged blue-white slabs, as if the sea had frozen solid in a single fierce moment.

“Imagine being there,” murmured Grandad, who had offered to bring her. “Months of cold. No easy way home.”

Samira pictured the real ship groaning under pressure from the ice. She imagined men stepping onto a frozen world that shone in every direction. It sounded brave, but also lonely.

She opened her sketchbook, yet instead of drawing the carved figurehead she began to copy the tiny ship. The more she looked, the more details she saw: crates on the deck, a little lifeboat, a flag no bigger than her thumb.

When a museum volunteer walked over, Samira asked if the expedition had succeeded.

“In some ways,” the woman replied. “They did not complete their original journey, but the crew survived.”

Samira nodded slowly. The answer stayed with her. Success, she thought, was not always about reaching the first goal. Sometimes it meant enduring long enough to find another way.

passages.title: What Are Polar Expeditions?
passages.text:
Polar expeditions are journeys made into the Earth's coldest regions: the Arctic in the north and Antarctica in the south. Explorers have travelled there to map coastlines, study wildlife, collect scientific information and test how humans survive in extreme conditions.

These journeys are difficult for many reasons. Temperatures can fall far below freezing, strong winds make it feel even colder, and thick ice can block ships from moving. During parts of the year, darkness may last for long periods, making work harder and affecting mood.

Explorers prepare carefully before setting off. They need warm layered clothing, strong tents, reliable food supplies and emergency equipment. Modern teams also use radios, satellite tools and weather forecasts, which make polar travel safer than it was in the past.

Even today, polar expeditions are demanding. However, the information gathered on these journeys helps scientists learn more about climate, oceans and the animals that live in these remote places.

passages.title: Every Bolt and Beam
passages.text:
The bridge over Mill Brook had always been there, or so it seemed. It was a simple footbridge: metal rails, wooden boards, peeling green paint. Most people crossed it without slowing down.

Not Eli.

Ever since the council had put up a notice saying the bridge would close for repairs, he had begun to study it properly. On his walk home, he stopped to look at the bolts holding the rails in place and the thick metal beams underneath. He pressed his shoe against one board and listened to its hollow knock.

On Saturday, he returned with a notebook.

His aunt Priya, who designed buildings, smiled when she saw him crouching by the stream. “Inspecting it?”

“Trying to understand it,” Eli said.

Priya pointed to the triangular metal shapes along the side. “Those help spread the weight,” she explained. “Bridges are all about forces. Push, pull, bend, hold.”

Eli wrote the words down carefully.

When a cyclist crossed, the bridge trembled slightly, but it did not seem weak. It seemed busy. Every piece was doing a job at the same time.

By the time they headed home, Eli had filled two pages. The repairs no longer felt annoying. They felt important. If the bridge was going to carry people safely for years, then every bolt, board and beam mattered.

passages.title: How Bridges Stay Strong
passages.text:
Bridges are built to help people and vehicles cross obstacles such as rivers, valleys or roads. Different kinds of bridges are used for different jobs, depending on the distance to cross, the weight to carry and the materials available.

A beam bridge is one of the simplest types. It uses a horizontal beam supported at each end. Small footbridges are often beam bridges. A truss bridge uses a pattern of triangles to spread forces through the structure. This helps the bridge stay strong while using less material.

Engineers must think carefully about forces when designing a bridge. Some parts are pushed together in compression, while others are pulled apart in tension. A successful design spreads these forces safely so that no single part takes too much strain.

Before a bridge is repaired or replaced, engineers inspect it closely. They check for rust, cracks, worn surfaces and weak joints. This keeps bridges safe and helps them last longer.

passages.title: The Square in Bloom
passages.text:
Martha had never liked arriving early, but on the morning of the community planting day she was the first child in the square. The benches were stacked with spades, gloves and trays of young plants with labels she could barely pronounce.

An orange-vested volunteer waved. “Would you mind helping sort the seedlings?”

Martha nodded, though she knew almost nothing about gardening.

Soon the square filled with people. A baker from the corner shop brought a crate of empty boxes for carrying compost. Two older boys fetched water from the tap by the church gate. A woman with a baby strapped to her chest knelt to loosen the soil around the flowerbeds.

At first, Martha kept to the edge, placing labels in neat rows. Then she noticed that one tray had been set in the wrong place. It contained shade-loving plants, yet the bed beside the fountain was in full sun. She remembered hearing the volunteer explain that different plants needed different conditions.

“Should these go near the wall instead?” she asked.

The volunteer looked surprised, then pleased. “Exactly right.”

After that, people kept asking Martha where things should go. By lunchtime, her knees were muddy and her ponytail had come loose, but the square looked transformed. The bare beds had become strips of green and silver. Bees were already nosing around the lavender.

As she packed away the labels, Martha realised she was no longer the child who had arrived too early. She felt like someone who belonged to the place she had helped change.

passages.title: Why Pollinators Matter
passages.text:
Pollinators are animals that carry pollen from one flower to another. This helps many plants produce seeds, fruit and new growth. Bees are among the best-known pollinators, but butterflies, moths, hoverflies and even some birds can do this job too.

Different pollinators are attracted to different kinds of flowers. Bright colours, strong scents and nectar are all useful in drawing them in. A garden with a variety of plants is more likely to attract a range of pollinators across the year.

People can support pollinators by planting flowers that bloom at different times, avoiding harmful chemicals and leaving some areas slightly wild. A shallow dish of water and a mix of sunny and sheltered spots can also help.

Pollinators are important not only for gardens but for farms and orchards as well. Without them, many crops would be harder to grow. Protecting pollinators helps both wild plants and the food people eat.

passages.title: The Delayed Train
passages.text:
The train was due at 9:14, but by 9:20 the platform display still flashed DELAYED in stubborn yellow letters. Jonah shifted his rucksack from one shoulder to the other and tried not to look annoyed.

Mum checked the timetable again. “There’s been a signalling problem.”

Across the tracks, a freight train rolled slowly past, every wagon covered in brown dust. Jonah watched it disappear beneath the footbridge. He had planned the whole day carefully: train to the city, river ferry to the science centre, then lunch before the robotics show. Delays did not fit the plan.

At last, their train arrived with a sigh of brakes. When they boarded, Jonah expected to stay cross, but the carriage windows framed everything like moving pictures. Warehouses slid into allotments; allotments gave way to terraces; terraces opened into a glitter of canal water.

Near the city, the train crossed a high iron bridge. Jonah looked down at roads, rooftops and tiny buses threading through the streets below. For a moment, the journey itself felt like part of the trip rather than a pause before it.

Mum smiled. “Worth waiting for?”

Jonah tried not to grin back. “Maybe a little.”

As the train curved into the station, he realised that transport was not just about arriving somewhere. It also stitched places together, carrying people from one kind of day into another.

passages.title: How Railways Work Safely
passages.text:
Railways are carefully organised systems that move people and goods from place to place. For trains to run safely, many parts of the system must work together, including tracks, signals, stations and timetables.

Signals are one important part of railway safety. They tell drivers when it is safe to move, slow down or stop. Modern railways may use coloured lights and computer systems, but the basic idea is the same: trains must be kept a safe distance apart.

Railways are used for more than passenger journeys. Freight trains carry materials such as stone, timber, fuel and containers of goods. Moving these items by rail can reduce the number of lorries needed on roads.

Although delays can be frustrating, they are sometimes caused by checks or repairs that help keep passengers safe. Weather, busy tracks and technical faults can also affect services.

passages.title: The Gust
passages.text:
On the second morning of the sailing course, the lake looked harmless. The surface was smooth and pale, and only the smallest ripples touched the jetty.

“That usually means the wind will change later,” said instructor Mae, checking the flags onshore.

Rafi was assigned to crew in the smallest training dinghy. He liked the boat's quickness, but not the way it tipped when he moved too suddenly. As they pushed off, he kept his weight perfectly still and watched the sail fill.

For the first few minutes, everything went well. The dinghy cut neatly across the lake. Then a gust swept down from the hill behind the trees.

The boom swung faster than Rafi expected. Water slapped the side of the boat. He grabbed the rope too late and the dinghy heeled sharply, sending cold spray across his sleeve.

“Ease the sheet!” called Mae from the safety boat.

Rafi obeyed, heart thumping. The sail loosened. The dinghy steadied.

By the time they reached the marker buoy, his breathing had slowed. He tried the movement again, this time earlier and more smoothly. The boat answered at once, no longer jerking but turning cleanly through the gust.

Back on shore, Rafi replayed the moment in his head. The gust had not vanished; he had simply learned to feel it sooner. Sailing, he realised, was less about forcing the boat to obey and more about noticing what the wind was already doing.

passages.title: How a Dinghy Sails
passages.text:
Sailing boats move by using the wind. When wind pushes against a sail, it creates a force that helps drive the boat forward. The shape of the sail and the angle of the boat both affect how well this works.

A small sailing boat, such as a dinghy, can react quickly to changes in wind. This can make dinghies exciting to sail, but it also means the sailor must respond quickly. If a sudden gust fills the sail too strongly, the boat may heel over, which means it leans sharply to one side.

Sailors control the sail using ropes called sheets. By pulling in or easing the sheet, they change the angle of the sail to suit the wind. Good sailing is not only about strength. It also depends on balance, timing and careful observation.

Learning to sail often involves practising the same movements many times. As sailors gain experience, they become better at spotting changes in wind and reacting before the boat becomes difficult to control.

passages.title: When the Lights Went Out
passages.text:
When the first stars appeared above the campsite, Poppy expected the usual things: torch beams, crackling wrappers, someone asking where the marshmallows were. She did not expect quiet.

“Power cut,” said Uncle Dan, holding up the dead lantern. “Battery pack too.”

For a moment the whole field seemed to pause. Then a child from the next tent pointed upward.

The sky was crowded.

Without the electric glow from the nearby visitor centre, hundreds more stars had appeared. A pale stripe stretched from one horizon to the other like brushed smoke.

“What’s that?” Poppy whispered.

“The Milky Way,” said Uncle Dan.

People who had been fussing over stoves and folding chairs began to stand still. Even the younger children stopped chasing one another and stared. Poppy lay back on the picnic blanket. Above her, the stars did not twinkle politely the way they did from her street at home. They blazed.

Soon, Uncle Dan used a paper map of the night sky to point out a bright planet and a crooked line of stars that made part of a constellation. Poppy tried to memorise everything, but the names mattered less than the feeling. The sky seemed enormous, yet strangely close, as if it had been waiting all evening for the campsite lights to disappear.

When the lantern finally flickered back to life, nobody seemed in a hurry to turn it on. The darkness no longer felt empty. It felt full.

passages.title: Seeing More Stars
passages.text:
On clear nights, people can often see stars, planets and patterns called constellations in the sky. However, the number of stars visible depends greatly on how dark the sky is.

Light pollution is caused by artificial light from streets, buildings and vehicles. In bright towns and cities, this extra light makes the sky look paler and hides many stars from view. In darker places, far more stars can be seen.

The Milky Way is the galaxy that contains our Solar System. From Earth, it can sometimes be seen as a faint band stretching across the sky. It is not always easy to spot, especially in places affected by light pollution.

People who enjoy observing the night sky are called astronomers if they study it scientifically, but many others simply enjoy stargazing as a hobby. A star chart or sky map can help people identify constellations and planets more easily.

passages.title: The Hidden Path 1
passages.text: Ella had walked through the woods many times, but today a narrow path between two silver-barked trees caught ella's eye. It curved away from the main track and disappeared under ferns. Although the place was familiar, the path felt secret, as if it had been waiting to be noticed. Ella followed it slowly, listening to the drip of water and the tapping of a woodpecker. At the end of the trail, a flat stone lay half buried in moss. Someone had carved an arrow into it. The arrow pointed towards a stream hidden behind brambles. On the other side stood a small wooden bridge. It looked old, but the boards were firm when ella tested them with one foot. Ella paused. Crossing would mean leaving the safe, known path behind. Yet the careful carving on the stone suggested that somebody once wanted travellers to continue. Taking a breath, ella stepped onto the bridge and listened as it answered with a soft creak.

passages.title: How Mountains Change 1
passages.text: Mountains are some of the tallest and most dramatic features on Earth. Some form when huge sections of the Earth's crust push against each other and buckle upwards. Others grow because volcanic material piles up around an opening in the ground. Although mountains appear solid and unchanging, they are slowly altered by weather and erosion. Rain can wear away rock, rivers can cut valleys and ice can carve sharp ridges. The climate on mountains also changes with height. It is usually colder and windier near the top, and fewer plants can survive there. Even so, mountain habitats can support specialised animals. People use mountains for farming, climbing, skiing and collecting water from snowmelt. Learning about mountains helps explain why landscapes differ so much from place to place.

passages.title: The Last Boat In 2
passages.text: By late afternoon, dark cloud had pressed low over the harbour, and the ropes tied to the posts had begun to hum in the wind. Noah was helping stack empty crates when a shout made everyone turn towards the sea. One fishing boat was still outside the harbour wall. It rose and dipped sharply between the waves. A man on the pier lifted a lamp and swung it in a wide circle. For a moment, the boat vanished behind a wall of spray, and noah's stomach tightened. Then it appeared again, slightly off line. 'Too far east,' muttered the harbour master. Without waiting to be asked, Noah ran to the signal pole and hauled on the line that lifted the orange marker higher. The fabric snapped in the wind. The boat turned. Slowly, carefully, it lined up with the harbour entrance and slid inside, leaving only churning foam behind. Only then did Noah realise how hard noah's heart had been beating.

passages.title: Roads of the Roman World 2
passages.text: Roman roads helped people, messages and supplies travel quickly across a large empire. Roman engineers chose routes carefully, often trying to make roads as straight as possible. They dug out the surface and filled the space with layers of stone and gravel. On top, they added tightly fitted paving stones to create a hard, long-lasting surface. Many roads had ditches at the sides to help rainwater drain away. These roads mattered because soldiers needed to move quickly, but roads also helped traders, officials and messages travel faster. In Britain, some modern roads still follow Roman lines because the original routes were so practical. Although the Roman Empire ended long ago, its roads remain a powerful example of careful planning and engineering.

passages.title: The Missing Brass Key 3
passages.text: In the old village hall, cupboards lined one wall from floor to ceiling. The smallest cupboard held the silver cups used for fairs and football matches, and its brass key had vanished. Everyone blamed untidiness, but Asha noticed things others ignored. The key had hung from a blue ribbon the day before. Now the ribbon lay on the floor near a bench, and a faint muddy mark led towards the back door. Outside, the yard was drying after rain. Wheel tracks from a delivery van crossed the ground, but one set of smaller footprints curved towards the shed. Asha followed them and found the door half open. Behind a stack of folded tables sat a box of jumble-sale books, a strip of blue ribbon, and, shining beneath it, the missing key. Asha smiled. Nobody had stolen it. Someone had used the ribbon by accident and dropped the key without noticing. Picking it up, asha hurried back, already imagining the surprised faces indoors.

passages.title: Ships of the Vikings 3
passages.text: Viking ships were carefully designed for long journeys and fast movement across water. Many were clinker-built, which means the wooden planks overlapped like scales. This made the hull strong but flexible enough for rough water. Longships were narrow and fast, with a sail for windy conditions and oars for calm water or careful steering. Their shallow draught meant they could travel not only across seas but also up rivers. Other Viking ships were wider cargo vessels used to carry goods such as wool, timber and tools. Building a ship required skill, teamwork and knowledge of wood, rope and weather. Archaeologists have learned a great deal about Viking travel by studying ship remains found in burial sites and mud.

passages.title: Night Wings 4
passages.text: Just after sunset, a thud came from the far corner of the garden. Ben looked up and saw a pale shape tangled in the netting that protected the strawberries. It was a young owl. Each time it tried to flap, the net pulled tighter around one wing. Ben remembered being told not to rush towards frightened animals, so ben moved slowly and spoke in a calm voice. A neighbour fetched thick gloves and a blanket. Together they covered the owl's head to keep it still. Under the blanket, its body felt surprisingly light. Ben worked at the knots one by one until the final strand slipped free. The owl did not fly away at once. Instead, it stood among the cabbages, blinking as if the garden had changed shape around it. Then it opened both wings, beat the air twice and lifted to the apple tree. From there, it launched itself silently into the darkening sky. Only when it had gone did Ben realise how tense the whole rescue had made ben feel.

passages.title: Why Animals Migrate 4
passages.text: Migration is the regular movement of animals from one place to another. Some travel to find food when seasons change, while others move to breed in safer places. Arctic terns make one of the longest migrations of any animal, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back again. Wildebeest in Africa travel in huge herds to follow fresh grass after rain. Migration can be dangerous. Animals may face storms, predators and the challenge of finding the correct route. Many species use clues such as the position of the sun, stars, coastlines or the Earth's magnetic field. Scientists study migration by attaching tiny trackers to animals so that their journeys can be mapped. Understanding migration is important because changes to climate and habitats can disrupt routes used for many years.

passages.title: The Rope Bridge Race 5
passages.text: On challenge day, each group had to cross an imaginary river without stepping on the grass. Teams were given two planks and three plastic crates. Most children rushed to begin, but Mina stopped to watch. Every time another team moved a crate too soon, the whole bridge collapsed. 'Let's plan first,' Mina said. At first the others groaned, sure that planning meant wasting time. Still, they listened. Mina suggested placing the crates in a zigzag so the planks overlapped more steadily. It took longer to set up, yet once the first child crossed, the bridge felt secure. Halfway through, one crate tilted. Amir wobbled and windmilled his arms. Without thinking, Mina dropped to one knee, held the plank steady and told everyone else to freeze. The movement stopped, the crate settled, and the group crossed one by one. They did not finish first, but when the teamwork award was announced, every face in mina's team lit up.

passages.title: Energy in a Food Chain 5
passages.text: A food chain shows how energy passes from one living thing to another. Most food chains begin with plants because plants make their own food using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide. An animal that eats plants is called a herbivore. A carnivore eats other animals, while an omnivore eats both plants and animals. Each step in the chain is known as a trophic level. When one part of a food chain changes, the effects can spread. If there are fewer insects, birds may struggle to find enough food. If a top predator disappears, the animals it once hunted may increase too much. In real habitats, food chains link together to form food webs, which are more complex. Studying them helps scientists understand balance in ecosystems.

passages.title: The Tree That Knew 6
passages.text: At the edge of the park stood an ash tree older than the swings, the benches and perhaps even the road beyond the gate. People said the wind sounded strange in its branches, though most laughed after saying so. Leo only half believed the stories until a gust bent the twigs low and a dry whisper brushed past leo's ear. Tucked into the bark was a smooth green marble, and beneath it a narrow crack opened like a tiny shelf. Inside lay three folded notes. The oldest asked for courage before a hospital visit. Another wished for rain for a neighbour's garden. The newest said simply, 'I hope Dad finds work soon.' Leo read them twice, feeling as though the tree had been trusted with worries too heavy to say aloud. After a long moment, Leo wrote a note asking for bravery before the assembly and slid it into the crack. As leo walked away, the leaves rustled again, and this time the whisper sounded almost like an answer.

passages.title: How Bridges Carry Weight 6
passages.text: Bridges help people and vehicles cross obstacles such as rivers, roads and valleys. Different bridge designs solve different problems. A beam bridge is simple and works well for shorter distances. An arch bridge uses a curved shape to spread weight into its supports. A suspension bridge hangs the road from strong cables, making it useful for long spans. Engineers must think carefully about forces. Compression squeezes materials, while tension pulls them apart. Wind, temperature and the movement of vehicles can all affect a bridge too. Before a bridge is built, engineers test ideas using models, calculations and computer designs. Good bridges do more than carry traffic: they connect communities, shorten journeys and can become famous landmarks.

passages.title: The Cave Below the Cliffs 7
passages.text: Low tide had uncovered a strip of black rock below the cliffs, and Ruby followed it farther than usual. Around one bend, hidden behind hanging roots, was a narrow opening that breathed out cold air. Ruby crouched and peered inside. The cave roof was low at first, then lifted suddenly, turning the space beyond into a dim grey hall. Drops fell from the ceiling and rang in small pools below. Ruby took three careful steps and saw pale shells pressed into the rock wall. Further in, someone had stacked flat stones into a tiny tower. It could have been old, or it could have been built that morning. The thought made ruby glance over one shoulder. The cave was fascinating, but it also felt watchful. When a stronger wave boomed outside the entrance, the noise rolled through the chamber like distant thunder. Ruby decided not to go deeper alone and hurried back into daylight, already planning to return with a torch and an adult.

passages.title: What Satellites Do 7
passages.text: Satellites are objects that travel around a planet or another body in space. Some are natural, like the Moon, while others are made by humans. Human-made satellites are launched into space on rockets and placed into carefully chosen orbits. Weather satellites take pictures of clouds and storms, helping forecasters predict conditions. Communication satellites send television, phone and internet signals between distant places. Navigation satellites help devices work out location by sending timing signals to receivers on the ground. There are also scientific satellites that study space, the atmosphere and the oceans. Engineers must make sure satellites can survive launch, extreme temperatures and the challenge of running on limited power. Many parts of everyday life now depend on them.

passages.title: The Folded Map 8
passages.text: Rain had cancelled football practice, so Tariq climbed to the attic to look for a board game. In a cracked suitcase under an old coat, tariq found a folded map tied with string. A red line wound from the village church to the mill pond and ended beside a drawing of an oak leaf. On the back were the words, 'For the finder with patient eyes.' It was probably a family game, yet the map looked serious enough to be real. By the time Tariq reached the pond, the rain had eased. Reeds whispered at the edge, and the oak tree spread its roots like giant fingers. After searching in the wrong places twice, tariq noticed a loose stone at the base of the trunk. Beneath it lay a tin box holding two old coins, a marble and a note from Grandad: 'Treasure is better when it is discovered than when it is bought.' Tariq laughed aloud, wishing Grandad could have seen the grin on tariq's face.

passages.title: Inside a Volcano 8
passages.text: Volcanoes are openings in the Earth's crust where hot rock, ash and gases can escape. Deep underground, rock can become so hot that it melts into magma. When pressure builds, magma can force its way upwards through cracks. If it reaches the surface, it is called lava. Some eruptions are explosive and send ash high into the air, while others are slower and allow lava to pour steadily down the sides of the volcano. Scientists watch volcanoes closely because small earthquakes, swelling ground and rising gases can all be warning signs. Volcanoes can be dangerous, yet they also shape the planet. Over time, lava cools to form new rock, and volcanic ash can make soil rich enough for plants to grow well. Studying volcanoes helps people understand the power of the Earth and prepare more safely for change.

passages.title: Stepping Stones 9
passages.text: The stepping stones across the river had always seemed easy when the water was low. Today, however, rain from the hills had turned the current swift and brown. Each stone wore a skirt of foam. Sofia needed to reach the meadow path on the far bank before the light faded, but the first jump suddenly looked much longer than usual. Kneeling by the bank, sofia watched the flow for a moment. Near each stone the water curled into calmer pockets. Holding a branch for balance, Sofia stepped onto the first stone and tested it. One by one, sofia moved across, never rushing. At the fourth stone, a blackbird burst from the reeds and sofia's arms flew up in surprise, but Sofia steadied again before taking the final leap. On the far bank, sofia looked back, proud not because the crossing had been easy, but because it had required calm thinking.

passages.title: Cities Beneath the Sea 9
passages.text: Coral reefs may look like colourful rocks, but they are built by tiny living animals called coral polyps. Each polyp makes a hard outer skeleton. As generations of polyps live and die, these skeletons build up into reefs. Warm, shallow seas are the best places for many reefs because sunlight can reach the water easily. This matters because coral often lives with tiny algae that use sunlight to make food. Reefs are important homes for fish, crabs and many other creatures, offering shelter and feeding grounds. They also protect coastlines by helping to break the force of waves. Rising sea temperatures, pollution and careless fishing can damage reef habitats. Scientists and local communities work together to protect these colourful ecosystems because reefs support both wildlife and people.

passages.title: The Empty Seat 10
passages.text: There had been an empty seat by the window for three days before the new boy arrived. When Daniel finally came in, he kept one hand on the strap of his bag as though he might need to leave again quickly. Mrs Khan introduced him and asked Jacob to show him where to put his reading book. At break, most children stayed with their usual groups. Daniel stood near the wall, watching a football game he clearly wanted to join but did not interrupt. Jacob knew that feeling. Last year, after moving house, jacob had spent a week pretending to prefer being alone. So instead of running off with friends, Jacob walked over and asked if Daniel wanted to help set up the cones for a passing game. Daniel's shoulders dropped a little, as if someone had removed a weight. He said yes at once. A few minutes later, when the ball bounced away, Daniel trapped it neatly with his foot and passed it back. By the time the bell rang, the empty seat no longer felt empty at all.

passages.title: The Journey of a River 10
passages.text: Rivers shape the land as they flow from high ground towards the sea. A river often begins as a spring, stream or melting snow high in hills or mountains. In its upper course, the water moves quickly, cutting into the land and carrying stones downhill. Further along, the valley widens and the river begins to bend. These bends are called meanders. In the lower course, the water usually moves more slowly and may spread out across a floodplain. Finally, the river reaches its mouth, where it enters a sea, lake or another river. Rivers provide water for homes and farms, support plants and animals, and have often been used for travel and trade. At the same time, rivers can flood after heavy rain, so engineers sometimes build defences to protect nearby towns.

passages.title: The Hidden Path 11
passages.text: Ella had walked through the woods many times, but today a narrow path between two silver-barked trees caught ella's eye. It curved away from the main track and disappeared under ferns. Although the place was familiar, the path felt secret, as if it had been waiting to be noticed. Ella followed it slowly, listening to the drip of water and the tapping of a woodpecker. At the end of the trail, a flat stone lay half buried in moss. Someone had carved an arrow into it. The arrow pointed towards a stream hidden behind brambles. On the other side stood a small wooden bridge. It looked old, but the boards were firm when ella tested them with one foot. Ella paused. Crossing would mean leaving the safe, known path behind. Yet the careful carving on the stone suggested that somebody once wanted travellers to continue. Taking a breath, ella stepped onto the bridge and listened as it answered with a soft creak.

passages.title: How Mountains Change 11
passages.text: Mountains are some of the tallest and most dramatic features on Earth. Some form when huge sections of the Earth's crust push against each other and buckle upwards. Others grow because volcanic material piles up around an opening in the ground. Although mountains appear solid and unchanging, they are slowly altered by weather and erosion. Rain can wear away rock, rivers can cut valleys and ice can carve sharp ridges. The climate on mountains also changes with height. It is usually colder and windier near the top, and fewer plants can survive there. Even so, mountain habitats can support specialised animals. People use mountains for farming, climbing, skiing and collecting water from snowmelt. Learning about mountains helps explain why landscapes differ so much from place to place.

passages.title: The Last Boat In 12
passages.text: By late afternoon, dark cloud had pressed low over the harbour, and the ropes tied to the posts had begun to hum in the wind. Noah was helping stack empty crates when a shout made everyone turn towards the sea. One fishing boat was still outside the harbour wall. It rose and dipped sharply between the waves. A man on the pier lifted a lamp and swung it in a wide circle. For a moment, the boat vanished behind a wall of spray, and noah's stomach tightened. Then it appeared again, slightly off line. 'Too far east,' muttered the harbour master. Without waiting to be asked, Noah ran to the signal pole and hauled on the line that lifted the orange marker higher. The fabric snapped in the wind. The boat turned. Slowly, carefully, it lined up with the harbour entrance and slid inside, leaving only churning foam behind. Only then did Noah realise how hard noah's heart had been beating.

passages.title: Roads of the Roman World 12
passages.text: Roman roads helped people, messages and supplies travel quickly across a large empire. Roman engineers chose routes carefully, often trying to make roads as straight as possible. They dug out the surface and filled the space with layers of stone and gravel. On top, they added tightly fitted paving stones to create a hard, long-lasting surface. Many roads had ditches at the sides to help rainwater drain away. These roads mattered because soldiers needed to move quickly, but roads also helped traders, officials and messages travel faster. In Britain, some modern roads still follow Roman lines because the original routes were so practical. Although the Roman Empire ended long ago, its roads remain a powerful example of careful planning and engineering.

passages.title: The Missing Brass Key 13
passages.text: In the old village hall, cupboards lined one wall from floor to ceiling. The smallest cupboard held the silver cups used for fairs and football matches, and its brass key had vanished. Everyone blamed untidiness, but Asha noticed things others ignored. The key had hung from a blue ribbon the day before. Now the ribbon lay on the floor near a bench, and a faint muddy mark led towards the back door. Outside, the yard was drying after rain. Wheel tracks from a delivery van crossed the ground, but one set of smaller footprints curved towards the shed. Asha followed them and found the door half open. Behind a stack of folded tables sat a box of jumble-sale books, a strip of blue ribbon, and, shining beneath it, the missing key. Asha smiled. Nobody had stolen it. Someone had used the ribbon by accident and dropped the key without noticing. Picking it up, asha hurried back, already imagining the surprised faces indoors.

passages.title: Ships of the Vikings 13
passages.text: Viking ships were carefully designed for long journeys and fast movement across water. Many were clinker-built, which means the wooden planks overlapped like scales. This made the hull strong but flexible enough for rough water. Longships were narrow and fast, with a sail for windy conditions and oars for calm water or careful steering. Their shallow draught meant they could travel not only across seas but also up rivers. Other Viking ships were wider cargo vessels used to carry goods such as wool, timber and tools. Building a ship required skill, teamwork and knowledge of wood, rope and weather. Archaeologists have learned a great deal about Viking travel by studying ship remains found in burial sites and mud.

passages.title: Night Wings 14
passages.text: Just after sunset, a thud came from the far corner of the garden. Ben looked up and saw a pale shape tangled in the netting that protected the strawberries. It was a young owl. Each time it tried to flap, the net pulled tighter around one wing. Ben remembered being told not to rush towards frightened animals, so ben moved slowly and spoke in a calm voice. A neighbour fetched thick gloves and a blanket. Together they covered the owl's head to keep it still. Under the blanket, its body felt surprisingly light. Ben worked at the knots one by one until the final strand slipped free. The owl did not fly away at once. Instead, it stood among the cabbages, blinking as if the garden had changed shape around it. Then it opened both wings, beat the air twice and lifted to the apple tree. From there, it launched itself silently into the darkening sky. Only when it had gone did Ben realise how tense the whole rescue had made ben feel.

passages.title: Why Animals Migrate 14
passages.text: Migration is the regular movement of animals from one place to another. Some travel to find food when seasons change, while others move to breed in safer places. Arctic terns make one of the longest migrations of any animal, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back again. Wildebeest in Africa travel in huge herds to follow fresh grass after rain. Migration can be dangerous. Animals may face storms, predators and the challenge of finding the correct route. Many species use clues such as the position of the sun, stars, coastlines or the Earth's magnetic field. Scientists study migration by attaching tiny trackers to animals so that their journeys can be mapped. Understanding migration is important because changes to climate and habitats can disrupt routes used for many years.

passages.title: The Rope Bridge Race 15
passages.text: On challenge day, each group had to cross an imaginary river without stepping on the grass. Teams were given two planks and three plastic crates. Most children rushed to begin, but Mina stopped to watch. Every time another team moved a crate too soon, the whole bridge collapsed. 'Let's plan first,' Mina said. At first the others groaned, sure that planning meant wasting time. Still, they listened. Mina suggested placing the crates in a zigzag so the planks overlapped more steadily. It took longer to set up, yet once the first child crossed, the bridge felt secure. Halfway through, one crate tilted. Amir wobbled and windmilled his arms. Without thinking, Mina dropped to one knee, held the plank steady and told everyone else to freeze. The movement stopped, the crate settled, and the group crossed one by one. They did not finish first, but when the teamwork award was announced, every face in mina's team lit up.

passages.title: Energy in a Food Chain 15
passages.text: A food chain shows how energy passes from one living thing to another. Most food chains begin with plants because plants make their own food using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide. An animal that eats plants is called a herbivore. A carnivore eats other animals, while an omnivore eats both plants and animals. Each step in the chain is known as a trophic level. When one part of a food chain changes, the effects can spread. If there are fewer insects, birds may struggle to find enough food. If a top predator disappears, the animals it once hunted may increase too much. In real habitats, food chains link together to form food webs, which are more complex. Studying them helps scientists understand balance in ecosystems.

passages.title: The Tree That Knew 16
passages.text: At the edge of the park stood an ash tree older than the swings, the benches and perhaps even the road beyond the gate. People said the wind sounded strange in its branches, though most laughed after saying so. Leo only half believed the stories until a gust bent the twigs low and a dry whisper brushed past leo's ear. Tucked into the bark was a smooth green marble, and beneath it a narrow crack opened like a tiny shelf. Inside lay three folded notes. The oldest asked for courage before a hospital visit. Another wished for rain for a neighbour's garden. The newest said simply, 'I hope Dad finds work soon.' Leo read them twice, feeling as though the tree had been trusted with worries too heavy to say aloud. After a long moment, Leo wrote a note asking for bravery before the assembly and slid it into the crack. As leo walked away, the leaves rustled again, and this time the whisper sounded almost like an answer.

passages.title: How Bridges Carry Weight 16
passages.text: Bridges help people and vehicles cross obstacles such as rivers, roads and valleys. Different bridge designs solve different problems. A beam bridge is simple and works well for shorter distances. An arch bridge uses a curved shape to spread weight into its supports. A suspension bridge hangs the road from strong cables, making it useful for long spans. Engineers must think carefully about forces. Compression squeezes materials, while tension pulls them apart. Wind, temperature and the movement of vehicles can all affect a bridge too. Before a bridge is built, engineers test ideas using models, calculations and computer designs. Good bridges do more than carry traffic: they connect communities, shorten journeys and can become famous landmarks.

passages.title: The Cave Below the Cliffs 17
passages.text: Low tide had uncovered a strip of black rock below the cliffs, and Ruby followed it farther than usual. Around one bend, hidden behind hanging roots, was a narrow opening that breathed out cold air. Ruby crouched and peered inside. The cave roof was low at first, then lifted suddenly, turning the space beyond into a dim grey hall. Drops fell from the ceiling and rang in small pools below. Ruby took three careful steps and saw pale shells pressed into the rock wall. Further in, someone had stacked flat stones into a tiny tower. It could have been old, or it could have been built that morning. The thought made ruby glance over one shoulder. The cave was fascinating, but it also felt watchful. When a stronger wave boomed outside the entrance, the noise rolled through the chamber like distant thunder. Ruby decided not to go deeper alone and hurried back into daylight, already planning to return with a torch and an adult.

passages.title: What Satellites Do 17
passages.text: Satellites are objects that travel around a planet or another body in space. Some are natural, like the Moon, while others are made by humans. Human-made satellites are launched into space on rockets and placed into carefully chosen orbits. Weather satellites take pictures of clouds and storms, helping forecasters predict conditions. Communication satellites send television, phone and internet signals between distant places. Navigation satellites help devices work out location by sending timing signals to receivers on the ground. There are also scientific satellites that study space, the atmosphere and the oceans. Engineers must make sure satellites can survive launch, extreme temperatures and the challenge of running on limited power. Many parts of everyday life now depend on them.

passages.title: The Folded Map 18
passages.text: Rain had cancelled football practice, so Tariq climbed to the attic to look for a board game. In a cracked suitcase under an old coat, tariq found a folded map tied with string. A red line wound from the village church to the mill pond and ended beside a drawing of an oak leaf. On the back were the words, 'For the finder with patient eyes.' It was probably a family game, yet the map looked serious enough to be real. By the time Tariq reached the pond, the rain had eased. Reeds whispered at the edge, and the oak tree spread its roots like giant fingers. After searching in the wrong places twice, tariq noticed a loose stone at the base of the trunk. Beneath it lay a tin box holding two old coins, a marble and a note from Grandad: 'Treasure is better when it is discovered than when it is bought.' Tariq laughed aloud, wishing Grandad could have seen the grin on tariq's face.

passages.title: Inside a Volcano 18
passages.text: Volcanoes are openings in the Earth's crust where hot rock, ash and gases can escape. Deep underground, rock can become so hot that it melts into magma. When pressure builds, magma can force its way upwards through cracks. If it reaches the surface, it is called lava. Some eruptions are explosive and send ash high into the air, while others are slower and allow lava to pour steadily down the sides of the volcano. Scientists watch volcanoes closely because small earthquakes, swelling ground and rising gases can all be warning signs. Volcanoes can be dangerous, yet they also shape the planet. Over time, lava cools to form new rock, and volcanic ash can make soil rich enough for plants to grow well. Studying volcanoes helps people understand the power of the Earth and prepare more safely for change.

passages.title: Stepping Stones 19
passages.text: The stepping stones across the river had always seemed easy when the water was low. Today, however, rain from the hills had turned the current swift and brown. Each stone wore a skirt of foam. Sofia needed to reach the meadow path on the far bank before the light faded, but the first jump suddenly looked much longer than usual. Kneeling by the bank, sofia watched the flow for a moment. Near each stone the water curled into calmer pockets. Holding a branch for balance, Sofia stepped onto the first stone and tested it. One by one, sofia moved across, never rushing. At the fourth stone, a blackbird burst from the reeds and sofia's arms flew up in surprise, but Sofia steadied again before taking the final leap. On the far bank, sofia looked back, proud not because the crossing had been easy, but because it had required calm thinking.

passages.title: Cities Beneath the Sea 19
passages.text: Coral reefs may look like colourful rocks, but they are built by tiny living animals called coral polyps. Each polyp makes a hard outer skeleton. As generations of polyps live and die, these skeletons build up into reefs. Warm, shallow seas are the best places for many reefs because sunlight can reach the water easily. This matters because coral often lives with tiny algae that use sunlight to make food. Reefs are important homes for fish, crabs and many other creatures, offering shelter and feeding grounds. They also protect coastlines by helping to break the force of waves. Rising sea temperatures, pollution and careless fishing can damage reef habitats. Scientists and local communities work together to protect these colourful ecosystems because reefs support both wildlife and people.

passages.title: The Empty Seat 20
passages.text: There had been an empty seat by the window for three days before the new boy arrived. When Daniel finally came in, he kept one hand on the strap of his bag as though he might need to leave again quickly. Mrs Khan introduced him and asked Jacob to show him where to put his reading book. At break, most children stayed with their usual groups. Daniel stood near the wall, watching a football game he clearly wanted to join but did not interrupt. Jacob knew that feeling. Last year, after moving house, jacob had spent a week pretending to prefer being alone. So instead of running off with friends, Jacob walked over and asked if Daniel wanted to help set up the cones for a passing game. Daniel's shoulders dropped a little, as if someone had removed a weight. He said yes at once. A few minutes later, when the ball bounced away, Daniel trapped it neatly with his foot and passed it back. By the time the bell rang, the empty seat no longer felt empty at all.

passages.title: The Journey of a River 20
passages.text: Rivers shape the land as they flow from high ground towards the sea. A river often begins as a spring, stream or melting snow high in hills or mountains. In its upper course, the water moves quickly, cutting into the land and carrying stones downhill. Further along, the valley widens and the river begins to bend. These bends are called meanders. In the lower course, the water usually moves more slowly and may spread out across a floodplain. Finally, the river reaches its mouth, where it enters a sea, lake or another river. Rivers provide water for homes and farms, support plants and animals, and have often been used for travel and trade. At the same time, rivers can flood after heavy rain, so engineers sometimes build defences to protect nearby towns.

passages.title: The Hidden Path 21
passages.text: Ella had walked through the woods many times, but today a narrow path between two silver-barked trees caught ella's eye. It curved away from the main track and disappeared under ferns. Although the place was familiar, the path felt secret, as if it had been waiting to be noticed. Ella followed it slowly, listening to the drip of water and the tapping of a woodpecker. At the end of the trail, a flat stone lay half buried in moss. Someone had carved an arrow into it. The arrow pointed towards a stream hidden behind brambles. On the other side stood a small wooden bridge. It looked old, but the boards were firm when ella tested them with one foot. Ella paused. Crossing would mean leaving the safe, known path behind. Yet the careful carving on the stone suggested that somebody once wanted travellers to continue. Taking a breath, ella stepped onto the bridge and listened as it answered with a soft creak.

passages.title: How Mountains Change 21
passages.text: Mountains are some of the tallest and most dramatic features on Earth. Some form when huge sections of the Earth's crust push against each other and buckle upwards. Others grow because volcanic material piles up around an opening in the ground. Although mountains appear solid and unchanging, they are slowly altered by weather and erosion. Rain can wear away rock, rivers can cut valleys and ice can carve sharp ridges. The climate on mountains also changes with height. It is usually colder and windier near the top, and fewer plants can survive there. Even so, mountain habitats can support specialised animals. People use mountains for farming, climbing, skiing and collecting water from snowmelt. Learning about mountains helps explain why landscapes differ so much from place to place.

passages.title: The Last Boat In 22
passages.text: By late afternoon, dark cloud had pressed low over the harbour, and the ropes tied to the posts had begun to hum in the wind. Noah was helping stack empty crates when a shout made everyone turn towards the sea. One fishing boat was still outside the harbour wall. It rose and dipped sharply between the waves. A man on the pier lifted a lamp and swung it in a wide circle. For a moment, the boat vanished behind a wall of spray, and noah's stomach tightened. Then it appeared again, slightly off line. 'Too far east,' muttered the harbour master. Without waiting to be asked, Noah ran to the signal pole and hauled on the line that lifted the orange marker higher. The fabric snapped in the wind. The boat turned. Slowly, carefully, it lined up with the harbour entrance and slid inside, leaving only churning foam behind. Only then did Noah realise how hard noah's heart had been beating.

passages.title: Roads of the Roman World 22
passages.text: Roman roads helped people, messages and supplies travel quickly across a large empire. Roman engineers chose routes carefully, often trying to make roads as straight as possible. They dug out the surface and filled the space with layers of stone and gravel. On top, they added tightly fitted paving stones to create a hard, long-lasting surface. Many roads had ditches at the sides to help rainwater drain away. These roads mattered because soldiers needed to move quickly, but roads also helped traders, officials and messages travel faster. In Britain, some modern roads still follow Roman lines because the original routes were so practical. Although the Roman Empire ended long ago, its roads remain a powerful example of careful planning and engineering.

passages.title: The Missing Brass Key 23
passages.text: In the old village hall, cupboards lined one wall from floor to ceiling. The smallest cupboard held the silver cups used for fairs and football matches, and its brass key had vanished. Everyone blamed untidiness, but Asha noticed things others ignored. The key had hung from a blue ribbon the day before. Now the ribbon lay on the floor near a bench, and a faint muddy mark led towards the back door. Outside, the yard was drying after rain. Wheel tracks from a delivery van crossed the ground, but one set of smaller footprints curved towards the shed. Asha followed them and found the door half open. Behind a stack of folded tables sat a box of jumble-sale books, a strip of blue ribbon, and, shining beneath it, the missing key. Asha smiled. Nobody had stolen it. Someone had used the ribbon by accident and dropped the key without noticing. Picking it up, asha hurried back, already imagining the surprised faces indoors.

passages.title: Ships of the Vikings 23
passages.text: Viking ships were carefully designed for long journeys and fast movement across water. Many were clinker-built, which means the wooden planks overlapped like scales. This made the hull strong but flexible enough for rough water. Longships were narrow and fast, with a sail for windy conditions and oars for calm water or careful steering. Their shallow draught meant they could travel not only across seas but also up rivers. Other Viking ships were wider cargo vessels used to carry goods such as wool, timber and tools. Building a ship required skill, teamwork and knowledge of wood, rope and weather. Archaeologists have learned a great deal about Viking travel by studying ship remains found in burial sites and mud.

passages.title: Night Wings 24
passages.text: Just after sunset, a thud came from the far corner of the garden. Ben looked up and saw a pale shape tangled in the netting that protected the strawberries. It was a young owl. Each time it tried to flap, the net pulled tighter around one wing. Ben remembered being told not to rush towards frightened animals, so ben moved slowly and spoke in a calm voice. A neighbour fetched thick gloves and a blanket. Together they covered the owl's head to keep it still. Under the blanket, its body felt surprisingly light. Ben worked at the knots one by one until the final strand slipped free. The owl did not fly away at once. Instead, it stood among the cabbages, blinking as if the garden had changed shape around it. Then it opened both wings, beat the air twice and lifted to the apple tree. From there, it launched itself silently into the darkening sky. Only when it had gone did Ben realise how tense the whole rescue had made ben feel.

passages.title: Why Animals Migrate 24
passages.text: Migration is the regular movement of animals from one place to another. Some travel to find food when seasons change, while others move to breed in safer places. Arctic terns make one of the longest migrations of any animal, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back again. Wildebeest in Africa travel in huge herds to follow fresh grass after rain. Migration can be dangerous. Animals may face storms, predators and the challenge of finding the correct route. Many species use clues such as the position of the sun, stars, coastlines or the Earth's magnetic field. Scientists study migration by attaching tiny trackers to animals so that their journeys can be mapped. Understanding migration is important because changes to climate and habitats can disrupt routes used for many years.

passages.title: The Rope Bridge Race 25
passages.text: On challenge day, each group had to cross an imaginary river without stepping on the grass. Teams were given two planks and three plastic crates. Most children rushed to begin, but Mina stopped to watch. Every time another team moved a crate too soon, the whole bridge collapsed. 'Let's plan first,' Mina said. At first the others groaned, sure that planning meant wasting time. Still, they listened. Mina suggested placing the crates in a zigzag so the planks overlapped more steadily. It took longer to set up, yet once the first child crossed, the bridge felt secure. Halfway through, one crate tilted. Amir wobbled and windmilled his arms. Without thinking, Mina dropped to one knee, held the plank steady and told everyone else to freeze. The movement stopped, the crate settled, and the group crossed one by one. They did not finish first, but when the teamwork award was announced, every face in mina's team lit up.

passages.title: Energy in a Food Chain 25
passages.text: A food chain shows how energy passes from one living thing to another. Most food chains begin with plants because plants make their own food using sunlight, water and carbon dioxide. An animal that eats plants is called a herbivore. A carnivore eats other animals, while an omnivore eats both plants and animals. Each step in the chain is known as a trophic level. When one part of a food chain changes, the effects can spread. If there are fewer insects, birds may struggle to find enough food. If a top predator disappears, the animals it once hunted may increase too much. In real habitats, food chains link together to form food webs, which are more complex. Studying them helps scientists understand balance in ecosystems.

passages.title: The Tree That Knew 26
passages.text: At the edge of the park stood an ash tree older than the swings, the benches and perhaps even the road beyond the gate. People said the wind sounded strange in its branches, though most laughed after saying so. Leo only half believed the stories until a gust bent the twigs low and a dry whisper brushed past leo's ear. Tucked into the bark was a smooth green marble, and beneath it a narrow crack opened like a tiny shelf. Inside lay three folded notes. The oldest asked for courage before a hospital visit. Another wished for rain for a neighbour's garden. The newest said simply, 'I hope Dad finds work soon.' Leo read them twice, feeling as though the tree had been trusted with worries too heavy to say aloud. After a long moment, Leo wrote a note asking for bravery before the assembly and slid it into the crack. As leo walked away, the leaves rustled again, and this time the whisper sounded almost like an answer.

passages.title: How Bridges Carry Weight 26
passages.text: Bridges help people and vehicles cross obstacles such as rivers, roads and valleys. Different bridge designs solve different problems. A beam bridge is simple and works well for shorter distances. An arch bridge uses a curved shape to spread weight into its supports. A suspension bridge hangs the road from strong cables, making it useful for long spans. Engineers must think carefully about forces. Compression squeezes materials, while tension pulls them apart. Wind, temperature and the movement of vehicles can all affect a bridge too. Before a bridge is built, engineers test ideas using models, calculations and computer designs. Good bridges do more than carry traffic: they connect communities, shorten journeys and can become famous landmarks.

passages.title: The Cave Below the Cliffs 27
passages.text: Low tide had uncovered a strip of black rock below the cliffs, and Ruby followed it farther than usual. Around one bend, hidden behind hanging roots, was a narrow opening that breathed out cold air. Ruby crouched and peered inside. The cave roof was low at first, then lifted suddenly, turning the space beyond into a dim grey hall. Drops fell from the ceiling and rang in small pools below. Ruby took three careful steps and saw pale shells pressed into the rock wall. Further in, someone had stacked flat stones into a tiny tower. It could have been old, or it could have been built that morning. The thought made ruby glance over one shoulder. The cave was fascinating, but it also felt watchful. When a stronger wave boomed outside the entrance, the noise rolled through the chamber like distant thunder. Ruby decided not to go deeper alone and hurried back into daylight, already planning to return with a torch and an adult.

passages.title: What Satellites Do 27
passages.text: Satellites are objects that travel around a planet or another body in space. Some are natural, like the Moon, while others are made by humans. Human-made satellites are launched into space on rockets and placed into carefully chosen orbits. Weather satellites take pictures of clouds and storms, helping forecasters predict conditions. Communication satellites send television, phone and internet signals between distant places. Navigation satellites help devices work out location by sending timing signals to receivers on the ground. There are also scientific satellites that study space, the atmosphere and the oceans. Engineers must make sure satellites can survive launch, extreme temperatures and the challenge of running on limited power. Many parts of everyday life now depend on them.

passages.title: The Folded Map 28
passages.text: Rain had cancelled football practice, so Tariq climbed to the attic to look for a board game. In a cracked suitcase under an old coat, tariq found a folded map tied with string. A red line wound from the village church to the mill pond and ended beside a drawing of an oak leaf. On the back were the words, 'For the finder with patient eyes.' It was probably a family game, yet the map looked serious enough to be real. By the time Tariq reached the pond, the rain had eased. Reeds whispered at the edge, and the oak tree spread its roots like giant fingers. After searching in the wrong places twice, tariq noticed a loose stone at the base of the trunk. Beneath it lay a tin box holding two old coins, a marble and a note from Grandad: 'Treasure is better when it is discovered than when it is bought.' Tariq laughed aloud, wishing Grandad could have seen the grin on tariq's face.

passages.title: Inside a Volcano 28
passages.text: Volcanoes are openings in the Earth's crust where hot rock, ash and gases can escape. Deep underground, rock can become so hot that it melts into magma. When pressure builds, magma can force its way upwards through cracks. If it reaches the surface, it is called lava. Some eruptions are explosive and send ash high into the air, while others are slower and allow lava to pour steadily down the sides of the volcano. Scientists watch volcanoes closely because small earthquakes, swelling ground and rising gases can all be warning signs. Volcanoes can be dangerous, yet they also shape the planet. Over time, lava cools to form new rock, and volcanic ash can make soil rich enough for plants to grow well. Studying volcanoes helps people understand the power of the Earth and prepare more safely for change.

passages.title: Stepping Stones 29
passages.text: The stepping stones across the river had always seemed easy when the water was low. Today, however, rain from the hills had turned the current swift and brown. Each stone wore a skirt of foam. Sofia needed to reach the meadow path on the far bank before the light faded, but the first jump suddenly looked much longer than usual. Kneeling by the bank, sofia watched the flow for a moment. Near each stone the water curled into calmer pockets. Holding a branch for balance, Sofia stepped onto the first stone and tested it. One by one, sofia moved across, never rushing. At the fourth stone, a blackbird burst from the reeds and sofia's arms flew up in surprise, but Sofia steadied again before taking the final leap. On the far bank, sofia looked back, proud not because the crossing had been easy, but because it had required calm thinking.

passages.title: Cities Beneath the Sea 29
passages.text: Coral reefs may look like colourful rocks, but they are built by tiny living animals called coral polyps. Each polyp makes a hard outer skeleton. As generations of polyps live and die, these skeletons build up into reefs. Warm, shallow seas are the best places for many reefs because sunlight can reach the water easily. This matters because coral often lives with tiny algae that use sunlight to make food. Reefs are important homes for fish, crabs and many other creatures, offering shelter and feeding grounds. They also protect coastlines by helping to break the force of waves. Rising sea temperatures, pollution and careless fishing can damage reef habitats. Scientists and local communities work together to protect these colourful ecosystems because reefs support both wildlife and people.

passages.title: The Empty Seat 30
passages.text: There had been an empty seat by the window for three days before the new boy arrived. When Daniel finally came in, he kept one hand on the strap of his bag as though he might need to leave again quickly. Mrs Khan introduced him and asked Jacob to show him where to put his reading book. At break, most children stayed with their usual groups. Daniel stood near the wall, watching a football game he clearly wanted to join but did not interrupt. Jacob knew that feeling. Last year, after moving house, jacob had spent a week pretending to prefer being alone. So instead of running off with friends, Jacob walked over and asked if Daniel wanted to help set up the cones for a passing game. Daniel's shoulders dropped a little, as if someone had removed a weight. He said yes at once. A few minutes later, when the ball bounced away, Daniel trapped it neatly with his foot and passed it back. By the time the bell rang, the empty seat no longer felt empty at all.

passages.title: The Journey of a River 30
passages.text: Rivers shape the land as they flow from high ground towards the sea. A river often begins as a spring, stream or melting snow high in hills or mountains. In its upper course, the water moves quickly, cutting into the land and carrying stones downhill. Further along, the valley widens and the river begins to bend. These bends are called meanders. In the lower course, the water usually moves more slowly and may spread out across a floodplain. Finally, the river reaches its mouth, where it enters a sea, lake or another river. Rivers provide water for homes and farms, support plants and animals, and have often been used for travel and trade. At the same time, rivers can flood after heavy rain, so engineers sometimes build defences to protect nearby towns.

passages.title: The Hidden Path 31
passages.text: Ella had walked through the woods many times, but today a narrow path between two silver-barked trees caught ella's eye. It curved away from the main track and disappeared under ferns. Although the place was familiar, the path felt secret, as if it had been waiting to be noticed. Ella followed it slowly, listening to the drip of water and the tapping of a woodpecker. At the end of the trail, a flat stone lay half buried in moss. Someone had carved an arrow into it. The arrow pointed towards a stream hidden behind brambles. On the other side stood a small wooden bridge. It looked old, but the boards were firm when ella tested them with one foot. Ella paused. Crossing would mean leaving the safe, known path behind. Yet the careful carving on the stone suggested that somebody once wanted travellers to continue. Taking a breath, ella stepped onto the bridge and listened as it answered with a soft creak.

passages.title: How Mountains Change 31
passages.text: Mountains are some of the tallest and most dramatic features on Earth. Some form when huge sections of the Earth's crust push against each other and buckle upwards. Others grow because volcanic material piles up around an opening in the ground. Although mountains appear solid and unchanging, they are slowly altered by weather and erosion. Rain can wear away rock, rivers can cut valleys and ice can carve sharp ridges. The climate on mountains also changes with height. It is usually colder and windier near the top, and fewer plants can survive there. Even so, mountain habitats can support specialised animals. People use mountains for farming, climbing, skiing and collecting water from snowmelt. Learning about mountains helps explain why landscapes differ so much from place to place.
